text.title: Technical Debt Management Rules
text.summary: Rules for maintaining a debt register, scoring entries, allocating paydown budget, and preventing gaming.

rule.statement: Every debt item must have: ID, type (code/design/test/infra/docs/process/dep), severity (1-5 rubric), interest (hours/week wasted), fix-cost (S/M/L/XL), and a "why we accepted this" line written at merge time, not retroactively.
rule.rationale: Items without documented context lose their rationale; future maintainers cannot assess whether the original trade-off still applies.

rule.statement: Require a minimum impact threshold to enter the register: the item must cost at least 30 min/week in interest (slower builds, extra manual steps, debugging time). Cosmetic or linter-fixable issues do not qualify.
rule.rationale: Over-classification floods the register and makes high-impact items invisible by burying them in noise.

rule.statement: Never close a debt item without a linked PR or commit. Treat register changes as code: require review, not just self-merge.
rule.rationale: Agents and developers mark items "closed" when files move or are renamed; only a real code change counts as paydown.

rule.statement: Rank by impact × severity / fix-cost. High-interest items (≥ 2 hours/week) jump priority regardless of fix complexity. Ignore stable old code; target files that change frequently (hotspot analysis).
rule.rationale: Paying down debt in files that never change has zero velocity benefit. Hotspot data from `code-maat` or `git log --stat` identifies where debt actually hurts.

rule.statement: Allocate a fixed sprint budget (e.g., 20% of capacity) to debt paydown and protect it like any committed feature. Never let debt PRs share scope with feature PRs.
rule.rationale: Mixed PRs make it impossible to bisect regressions and obscure the true cost of debt work in velocity metrics.

list.item: 1 — Cosmetic: no measurable impact on velocity or operations
2 — Minor: adds < 30 min/week overhead; workaround exists
3 — Moderate: adds 30 min–2 h/week; no clean workaround
4 — High: adds > 2 h/week or caused ≥ 1 production incident
5 — Critical: blocks releases or poses security/compliance risk

antipattern.description: Closing items by renaming or splitting them without a code change.
antipattern.reason: Phantom resolution inflates "closed" metrics without reducing actual interest paid. The problem persists under a new name.

antipattern.description: Proposing a big-bang rewrite to pay down debt in one PR.
antipattern.reason: Large rewrites are hard to review, risky to deploy, and frequently abandoned halfway. Incremental paydown that reduces interest by 50% per step is more reliable.

antipattern.description: Agents bump every item to Severity=High without evidence.
antipattern.reason: Inflation makes the register useless for prioritization. Require an incident link or measured time-waste for any Severity 4-5 claim.
